author: @kaboom-zf2bl
text:
I have watched this playlist to this point so far ... essentially back to front of the development ... the 30 to 35 deg fins are great ( i would expect 33.333 degrees to b optimal)... now what about also angling the fins at 30 to 35 degrees to force the wind that IS captured to spin down the tube allowing more more volume to flow ... essentially taking victor schauberger's ideas for water and making a toroidial air flow ... make the angle match the spin to the direction of the turbines spin so if the turbine is a right hand spin then right hand fins ... left handed ... left hand fins  .... the panels can all stay flat and large scale construction then is flat bits with some compound cuts to fit the bits together ... also if you apply jet turbine exhaust maths (dang aeronautics LOL) you can fine tune the constriction to get an optimized air flow for the best performance of the turbine ... 
.
another consideration would be using magnetic bearings for the fan base and its spindle ... 
.
also if you want to include multiple turbines then a series of converging and diverging pipe section between the fans will both slow/ cool and then speed up and heat the air ... each turbine after the first oen would need to be some ratio smaller than the previous one ... eg if the airflow is left to right and you want say 3 turbines in line then the right hand turbine will be say 1unit the next one in will be 1.618 units ... then for the third ... same calculation BUT the 3rd one is 1.618 times larger than the 2nd ... PHI being such a common natural optimization ... 
.
also having reread this post ... what about using the coriolis effect on the cap to also pull the pass over air down into the back side of the collector increasing the air intake even more or have the weather vain shield protrude above the top a slight bit and curve the passing air down into the downwind side of the collector ... as for ease of construction a suitably sized round pipe for the top of the shroud should be good enough to accomplish this

text:
I think most people keep forgetting that air is a gaseous "fluid" so pressure and such effects it too, thus that idea of it not working down the pipe, is forgetting that you are stacking your pressure from the top pushing constantly into the pipe so the stuff already at the bottom gets pushed out. 

Maybe the surface of the interior could be made to be more effective and cause less drag, or something but for this, I think the effort would not hour of labor vs watts able to be produced be worth it due to diminishing returns on one's investment. Just build more of them in the time to make the first one more efficient LOL.

author: @RandomAxeOfKindness

Probably the thirtieth person to say this, but I can't possibly read all the comments:  The tower is basically a separated stack of plastic bowls with the bottoms cut out.  If you can find cheap plastic bowls with flat bottoms, cut the bottoms out and stack them with vertical supports that separate them.  It'd be interesting to see how much separation is ideal.

Generally, though, it seems easier to make louver rings and add vertical supports than to make so many individual segments and connect them.  You could, of course, cut a series of curves from a sheet of plastic, or metal flashing, and then bend them into circles.

author: @robgad2271
text:
Who says it won't work through a pipe?  Don't they know that the air handling heating and cooling system in their house or office is just big plumbing? - Seeing this video made me realize that three improvements will make this better, and namely it will be better in a large form mounted in my old three meter aluminum satellite dish as a huge collection end of the contraption.

A little history here, I am a retired HVAC master tech with a specialty on duct design.  There are a few key elements that one must follow on a natural velocity multiplier (this is what this should be called), and should have an ideal limit to the largest version being about half a meter in diameter with a quarter meter diameter duct with no bends of interruptions before it empties at a output manifold.

Natural wind is not a laminar flow, it fluctuated quite a lot, but this design and the math it seems ti indicate will cure a lot of those natural open air problems and produce a 0.5 velocity increase, captured mostly by the conversion to a better constant flow into the duct.  It should also work as a low flow evaporation cooler as well, operating with no blower or moving parts other than a small pump to the cooling pad.  Brilliant device.

author: @Digidoc316

While the device will work on a horizontal plane, I question the functionality of it mounted to a vertical pipe due to the weight of the air column within the pipe.  Also, there is a concern with the capture of rain shorting the generator at the bottom.
A possible solution would be to mount the generator beneath the device and use an extension shaft to a blade wheel positioned in the same plane as the exhaust outlet.
To increase flow, a tapered cone could be placed over the shaft to direct the flow upwards. Putting a wind vane exhaust head on the output would decrease the pressure at the outlet, boosting flow.

That's very cool!

One application for this might be for moisture harvesting.  In Peru and parts of Africa, they're building Fog Nets.  There have been other forms of Air Wells in Persia and Ukraine.

Having a large number of these or even a wind screen which would direct moist air underground into a water collection chamber would be interesting, especially if it could minimize the use of polyester and other imported materials in favor of Terra Cotta or wood.

One section of a "Darwin Wall" or several Darwin collectors could collect moist air to go underground either in pipes or to an underground cistern, and a solar Chimney with a Darwin Turbine on top could use both wind and solar heat to pull air up using both wind and solar heat with the moist air condensing below ground.

A system like that could be used for hundreds of years.  The 3d printed plastic molds could be used as forms for these projects.

I that's a concept bigger than just electric generation.  It could create suction for underground tubes and provide electric free geothermal heating and AC.

Very cool!

author: @jimstegman7396
text: You might try a converging nozzle just before the fan. The nozzle will trade off the pressure of the air flow for an increase in velocity. However an increase in velocity will increase drag, so do not place the nozzle near your Darwin device, but at the end of the pipe just before the fan. Increasing velocity at the expense of cross sectional area is a big win for power. The velocity has a greater effect on power than cross sectional area. Area affects the power directly. That is, double the area you double the power. However velocity is related to power on a CUBED basis... Double the velocity, the power increases by 8X. The only drawback of a converging nozzle is you need to keep the nozzle velocity less than sonic speed. (which I doubt will ever happen in this application) If you go up to (and beyond) sonic speed, a converging-diverging nozzle works better.

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
You might like to stack torricelli cones. 2d Y=(outer diameter)/x  where x is outer diameter and less, or 3D, Z=(outer diameter)/sqr(x²+y²).... 2D, y=1/x, where either X or Y is the outer diameter or less. 

Cones with a continual change  in pitch starting near parallel with the wind to near parallel with the pipe will help the air flow transition with minimal turbulence inside the pipe, with the added benefit of having a larger cross section input (outside) gradually converted to a smaller cross section output (inside) such that it increases the velocity without increasing the pressure inside  much, and not having a high pressure inside reduces the amount of air that flows around your structure instead.

Designes with high turbulence inside create a bottle neck that restricts air flow.
